metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: is going to somehow cater to this underlying distribution, D’s assumption that we are making, and that will lead us to different sorts of algorithms.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So, now what kind of assumptions we can make on this underlying distribution D? We know it is unknown, we know it is fixed. So, this is a common assumption, assumption number 1, which we are making. In addition to this, can we make more some more structural assumptions on the distribution D itself? So, that is where there are two major schools of thoughts, which lead us to two

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: major schools of thoughts, which lead us to two different types of modeling itself. So, what are these two types of modeling? Let us see that now.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: (Refer Slide Time: 8:47)

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So, we are still in the classification setup. We can think of similar things for the regression also, but then I will talk about the classification examples. So, the two major schools of thoughts with when it comes to modeling are the following. One is what is called as a generative model and we will talk about what it means in a minute and the other is what is called as a

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: in a minute and the other is what is called as a discriminative model. Remember, we are somehow trying to make further assumptions on the underlying distribution D over the feature , label space that generates our training and test data.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So, there are two different ways to do this modeling. One modeling is the generative model. The other modeling is what is called as the discriminative model. I will first tell you what these models are, what is the subtle but very key difference between these two models are and then we will talk about what does it mean in practice to say you are doing a generative

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: model or a discriminative model, and that will give us some insights into why people have two different types of modeling.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So, here is the key difference between generative model and discriminative model. So, you have a distribution over X and Y. So what does that even mean? That means that there is some probability or a density associated with every feature, and label, so there is some P(x,y). That is, if I tells you hey, this is my feature vector, and this is the corresponding label, then we should

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: this is the corresponding label, then we should be able to associate it with the density or a probability. Density, when your features are continuous; probability, when your features are discrete.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: Why? Because we have discussed that. So, when you have continuous random variables, then the probabilities for any feature vector will become 0, that is useless. So, we will model the density. So, using a density. So, this is a generative model. So if you are putting, if you are making assumptions on P(x,y), how should this density look like? Then it means that we are doing a

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: look like? Then it means that we are doing a generative modeling. I will talk about why it is called generative modeling in a bit, once I described what is the discriminative model.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: The discriminative model, on the other hand, models, not P(x,y), that means it does not model the joint distribution of features and labels. But it only models P(y|x). So, this guy models P(x,y) discriminative models, P(y|x). So, this is the fundamental difference. So either modeling the joint distribution of x , y, or just modeling P(y|x). Now what does it even mean to do say

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: P(y|x). Now what does it even mean to do say that there are two different types of modeling?

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: To understand this better, let us take a simple example. I am going to give this example from my point of view. But from your point of view, you can think of an analogous example very easily once you see what the example is. Let us say I am given the task to do the following. It

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: is a binary classification task where I will be given some spoken sentence and I need to say whether the sentence is in Bengali or not. This is the task that I am given.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: I am taking Bengali as an example, because I do not know how to speak or write Bengali. That is why I am taking this as an example. So, it is a binary classification example where I am given this task where, by looking at the audio signal of some sentence, I need to classify it as whether it is a Bengali sentence or a non-Bengali sentence. Now, of course, I have a lot of training

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: Now, of course, I have a lot of training data and from which I need to train a model. So, now I need to make some assumptions about how this data could have been generated.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So now, here is where the generative model and the discriminative model differs. The generative modelers who tell you that model, how Bengali sentences themselves are generated. So, in the sense that you are modeling x, x here is a feature vector, which is this Bengali sentence that is being given to me, if you are modeling how the features themselves are generated, and how the

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: features themselves are generated, and how the labels are generated, given the features. So, you are doing both of these, you are doing a joint modeling of both features and the labels, then it is called generative modeling.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: It means that I am making some assumptions about how Bengali sentences are generated. So, that is the key assumption in a generative model, where you are also modeling the feature generation process. On the other hand, the discriminative modelers who tell me, your goal is only to make sure whether the given sentence is Bengali or not Bengali. That is, your goal is to discriminate

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: Bengali. That is, your goal is to discriminate whether a given sentence is of a particular class or of a different class. In this particular case, it is a Bengali class or a non-Bengali class.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: To learn, to be able to acquire this ability to discriminate between Bengali or non-Bengali sentences, you do not necessarily need to know how the Bengali language sentences themselves are generated. So, you do not need to know how to write or read Bengali to generate Bengali sentences, to just discriminate whether a sentence is in Bengali or not. That is, if you only want to say,

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: Bengali or not. That is, if you only want to say, what is the y label, given a feature x, you only want to model how is y given x looking like. So we only want to model this in a probabilistic fashion, we do not have to care about how the x itself is generated.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: The generative modelers would say no, no, no, you should also care about how the x is generated, how the features are generated. So, this is the subtle difference. If you are also modeling the feature generation somehow, then it is a generative model; if you are only

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: caring about how to discriminate whether a given feature is in label y equal to 1 or y equal to -1, then that is a discriminative model. Now, one might ask, which is a better model?

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So now, I, as a person who does not know how to speak Bengali, will still be able to do reasonably well given a sentence to say whether it is Bengali or not, but if you asked me to speak in Bengali, I will not be able to do at all because I am not trained in Bengali. Now, let us ask another person who actually can speak Bengali and give that person the same task. Now that person

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: give that person the same task. Now that person is going to ace this task, because that person has the ability to generate Bengali sentences. So, this task is going to be very, very easy for that person as compared to me.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: Now, what does this mean? This tells us that now that person who can speak Bengali has a mental model of how to generate Bengali sentences, so that person knows the generative process itself. And so it becomes easy for that person to do a discriminative task. The task is at the end of the day to discriminate given a sentence Bengali or not. So, if you know how to generate then you

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: or not. So, if you know how to generate then you are able to discriminate well. So, on the other hand, if I try to model using my, if I try to force a mental model of the generative process of Bengali into my mind, now, that is not going to work because I do not know how to speak or write Bengali.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So, the high level takeaway then is the following. If you have reasons to believe how the exact features are generated in your task at hand, then it is a good idea to do generative modeling. Because if you know how to generate correctly, then you are usually better off in doing a discriminative task better as well. On the other hand, if you have no idea how the features are

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: hand, if you have no idea how the features are generated, then it may not be a good idea to do a generative modeling because if your model is wrong, then you would be suffering even in the discriminative task as well.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So, if your domain expertise is such that you know exactly how the data could have been generated, the features could have been generated, then generative modeling is a good idea. Otherwise, discriminative modeling is a good idea. So, at a high level, this is the takeaway that I want you to think about for now. Now, what we are going to do is we are going to look at a lot of

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: going to do is we are going to look at a lot of examples of algorithms, which makes specific generative assumptions and specific discriminative assumptions and then we will be able to appreciate this even better. So, when should you do a generative assumption versus when you should do discriminative assumptions?

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: There is no single answer to this because both are typically useful, but the broad idea is that if you do not have much idea about how the features are generated, then it is better to stick to a discriminative modeling based assumption. So, which is what a lot of algorithms that we will see, this course will also do. In fact, that brings us to this point that we have already kind

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: brings us to this point that we have already kind of seen two algorithms. Now we can ask the question whether these are generative model based assumptions or discriminative model based assumptions. what are the two algorithms we saw?

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: One is k nearest neighbor, the other is decision tree. Now in both these algorithms, we have a model in decision tree, you have a model; in nearest neighbors, the model is the entire data set. Nevertheless, if I want to call that a model, in both these algorithms, the commonality is that, I am not bothered about how the data features themselves was generated. The only thing I am

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: themselves was generated. The only thing I am bothered about is if a new feature is given, or if a feature is given, how do I model what the label for this is. In the nearest neighbor case, I would say that, for every data point, the label is based on the K nearest neighbors.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So, I do not care about how the x itself is generated. I am only specifying how to make a prediction of y if x is given. So, essentially we are doing discriminative modeling and nearest neighbor. The same thing in decision tree as well. I do not model how x is generated, I do not have any distributional assumption or any assumption as to how the data features are generated. But I

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: as to how the data features are generated. But I make the assumption that, if a feature is given, then the decision tree would be, traversing through the decision tree would give me the label.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So, I am able to discriminate whether a given feature is y equals 1 or y equals -1, using the decision tree that I have built from data. So, both these that we have seen so far, our K nearest neighbors and decision tree are examples of discriminative models.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: But then one asks the question, what is the probability in this? Where is probability coming into the picture? Because if I am going to say P(y|x), I need to make some probabilistic assumption. So, what is the probability here? This this is not really a probability, but then you can think of it as a trivial probability. So, in the sense that what would the probability corresponding

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: that what would the probability corresponding to this entry would be? So, it will say probability of y equals 1 given some x, so the class that the given test given point x belongs to this class 1, this probability is 1 if you know the decision tree for x says 1.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: Otherwise, this probability is 0 which means the decision tree would have said 0 or -1 depending on what your label is. So, this is a trivial probability. So, the probability can be either 1 or 0, which means that there is a deterministic decision that we are making for every data point. So, that is why I mean we can think of always probability as like a generalization of your

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: probability as like a generalization of your deterministic decision making. Similarly, in K nearest neighbors, you would say that probability of y equals 1 given x is 1 if majority of neighbors say 1 and the probability is 0 otherwise. Same thing is here, probability is 0 if that condition does not hold.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: So, these are deterministic mappings from input space to the output space. Input space is the features, output space is +1 or -1 or +1 or 0. So, all deterministic mappings fall under this category of P(y|x). So, because you are mapping given an x, you are just saying what the y is. So, we have seen already two examples, implicitly they are trivially discriminative models.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: What we are going to do next is look at an example for a generative model. So, we will look at one algorithm where which makes a generative modeling assumption and then it will try to

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: learn some nice interesting models once this assumption is put down carefully. So, the next thing that we are going to see is this. So, this is what we will see next, an example for a generative model based algorithm.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Generative and Discriminative Models**

text: Welcome back everybody, we are around at the midpoint of this course, around 6 weeks are over now. And so far, we have looked at unsupervised learning methods, we have looked at supervised learning, we have started to look at supervised learning, specifically we have looked in detail the problem of regression including linear regression, the non-linear version of it and then

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: regression, the non-linear version of it and then several variants including ridge regression and lasso.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: (Refer Slide Time: 0:57)

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: And what we are going to do from now on is look at another important paradigm in machine learning which is also supervised learning paradigm, but then the problem that we are going to look at is much more popular and I mean much more applied. And this is the problem of classification and this also falls under supervised learning as we have seen earlier. So, we will start looking

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: we have seen earlier. So, we will start looking at the problem of classification.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: As we saw the classification problem can be posed in two different ways, one is the standard binary classification where you only have two classes or the multi-class classification where you have several classes. So, what we are going to do in this course is look at binary classification because that is the most basic and important form of classification and once we understand

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: form of classification and once we understand binary classification well then we will see how ideas from binary classification can also be used most of the time seamlessly to solve the multi-class classification problem as well.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, our focus for the next couple of weeks is going to be on binary classification. So, what is the problem of binary classification? You have a data set x1,...,xn which is a bunch of features as usual all xi's are in d dimensional features but in addition because it is supervised learning we know that we will also have labels which is y1 to yn.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: And because it is a classification problem as opposed to a regression problem which we have dealt so far. Now, our labels are going to be either you can think of it as 0, 1 or you can think of it as -1, 1. So, essentially you have some features and then you want to box it into two different, two different classes.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, you can call these class as 0 or 1 or -1 or 1 does not really matter most of the times I mean depending on some mathematical convenience in some algorithms we will think of it as 0, 1, some other, some other algorithms we might think of it as -1, 1. But absolutely it does not matter because it is just a number that we are assigning to these classes.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, for example your problem could be something like given an email you want to figure out if the email is spam or not in which case your classes are just spam or non-spam and now you can without loss of generality say spam is 0, non-spam is 1 or spam is 1, non-spam is 0 or spam is 1, non-spam is -1, does not really matter. So, the main point is that there are two classes and

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: the main point is that there are two classes and hence it is called binary classification.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, what is the goal? The goal now is to do the following like in the regression case we still want to learn some function which maps Rd which is the feature space to let us I mean for this purpose of discussion let me just say 0 to 1. So, you want to learn a function from data which maps a new feature, any given feature to 0 or 1.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text:
Now what is the loss or the error that we are interested in. Remember in the regression problem the error that we were interested in is was the squared error and then we justified it in different ways. So, what might be an error that we might be interested in the classification case?  
(Refer Slide Time: 3:59)

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text:
(Refer Slide Time: 3:59)  
This is a common on very intuitive loss or error of a given function which could be as the 𝑛 following. So, loss of h, so this is the loss or error of h, we can think of it as ∑ maybe I will 𝑖=1  
do an averaging indicator of h(xi)≠yi, where the indicator function takes some argument z and outputs 1 if z is true and 0 otherwise.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, which means that basically what we are measuring is what is the fraction of points in the training set that our h gets wrong. So, when it gets it wrong you penalize it by value of 1, if it gets it right you do not penalize it the value is 0, so that is what this indicator is capturing and then we are dividing it by n, so we are basically counting the fraction of errors in our

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: basically counting the fraction of errors in our data set.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, this is a much more reasonable loss function than a square loss simply because what we really care about is whether you are doing it correctly or incorrectly. So, that is the main goal of classification to get as much data points correctly as possible in the test set. So, as a proxy we are going to measure it in the training setup.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: Of course, the same issue as regression would come in, so if you let all functions, if you let we are searching in the space of all functions then we will always be able to make this error 0 but then we would, we would have perhaps fit the noise as well which is the same argument we have made for regression.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, we should not be searching in the space of all functions, we should search in a space of restricted functions. And like linear, like regression where we said that the restricted class is a linear class, we can try to do the same thing for classification as well. So, what we could do is

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text:
we can then ask well I restrict my space of functions to just the linear functions and then my goal would be to, my algorithm would be to do the following.  
Minimize over h in H linear and I will tell you what H linear in this case means it is slightly  
𝑛 different from your usual regression case because of the nature of prediction, ∑ 1(h(xi) ≠yi). 𝑖=1

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: What is H linear here? What do I mean by H linear?

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: h linear, by H linear I mean the following. So, H linear is the set of all hypothesis functions or the classifiers h such that h of x, hw let us say it is characterized by some w such that hw(x) is the sign of w<sup>T</sup> x. So, where the sign is just our usual function which says sign of w<sup>T</sup> x or in general sign of any input z is going to be 1 if z ≥ 0 and 0

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: of any input z is going to be 1 if z ≥ 0 and 0 otherwise.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, basically you are still linearly combining the features by doing w<sup>T</sup> x and then asking well what is the sign of this quantity. So, you are thresholding it at 0, if your sign ≥ 0 then we are saying that it is the class that we will predict that h will predict is positive otherwise it is negative.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, in pictures this is going to look like the following. So, you have some w, so this corresponds to this gives rise to a function hw which is going to predict +1 if the for every x such that w<sup>T</sup> x is positive. But where are those x? We know where those x's are so those x's are in here.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, on the right hand side so to say of our hyper, hyperplane or in this case the line. So, this is the set of all x such that w<sup>T</sup> x ≥ 0. And similarly, you have the set of all, the other side is the set of all x such that w<sup>T</sup> x ≤ 0 which we have seen already, so this is just the set of all x such that w<sup>T</sup> x ≤ 0.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: And here is where this h will predict negative and the yellow region is where the h is going to predict positive. Of course, what happens if a point lies on the line it does not really matter because you can either predict positive or negative by but our definition says that we are going to predict it as negative without loss of generality.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: In some sense you can, if you want to switch the definition you can do that also, you can think of on the line you want to say positive as opposed to negative does not really matter or you can toss a coin, you can break dice somehow. So because, I mean w<sup>T</sup> x = 0 means it is tied, both the classes are tied and then you can decide it however you want.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text:
(Refer Slide Time: 9:32)  
So, now the question comes how do we solve this problem? Remember again, in the, going back to the regression case what we did was we said that we are going to find that w which minimizes our squared loss and then we started taking the derivative and then setting it to 0 and all those things we did.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: Now, the question is, now we have changed the loss, it is no longer the square loss, it is the, it is what is called as a 0-1 loss let me give this loss a name this loss is called the 0-1 loss because it takes, it gives a loss of either 0 or 1 depending on whether h correctly predicts x or not with respect to the y in the training set.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: Now, how do we minimize this? Unfortunately, this problem is an NP-hard problem in general, in general. What do I mean by in general? In general, for a general data set so we will see cases where this is easy to solve but then in general if I give you a data set xi, yi and ask you to find that test line which suffers the minimum loss with respect to this data it is hard to solve,

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: with respect to this data it is hard to solve, so it is an NP-hard problem, we do not know if there is an efficient algorithm to find such a w at all.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, while in the regression case it was very easy, we could just take the gradient, set it to 0 and solve the problem, here it is not so easy. So, that is because of the nature of the loss itself, regression was giving like a continuous loss function. So, depending on how much you are away from the label yi which is a real number there your loss kind of takes any possible value

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: there your loss kind of takes any possible value from 0 to infinity.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: Here the loss can take only two values, discrete values, so either 0 or 1 and this nature of, this discrete nature of this loss makes this loss function itself non differentiable and non-continuous and so we suffer this problem. So, this becomes like in some sense a discrete search problem and so it becomes NP-hard in general.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, we cannot solve this problem, so we have to give up on this. So, we have to look for, though this is what we want to do we have to look for alternate ways to solve the binary classification problem. So, how can we solve that? The first thing that immediately comes to our mind is well we already know how to solve the regression problem.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, in regression the labels could take any real value, here the labels are constrained to take 0 or 1 so it appears so that we have solved a more broader problem in the regression case, why cannot we simply use that idea to solve this problem. So, that is the question we are going to ask next and see if that will be of any use at all.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, can we use let us say linear regression to solve classification problems. By which what do I mean? I mean the following, so you are given a data set (x1, y1),...,(xn, yn), you input the data set to a linear regression model and what you get is a w, so in the standard linear regression this is equivalent to the maximum likelihood and all that, we know that.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, let us say you get some w in Rd and now you use the w to get hw by thresholding w at w<sup>T</sup> x = 0, hw is from Rd to 0-1. Is this a good idea that is the question we are asking? So, now of course this is not solving the 0-1 loss, this is solving a square loss, so for now let us think of

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: this as a vanilla version where we do not have regularization, adding regularization would not change our argument so much.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: But let us say for the moment we assume that this is a non-regularized linear regression, the standard version, where you are just minimizing for the square loss. So, what is going to happen here? So, that is the question we are asking. You could do this but there is a problem.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: Now, just to illustrate this problem let us take this data set. So, here is a data set which has bunch of let us say positive labels which I am calling as, which I am marking using the red dots and let us say we have a point, some points here which are green dots, which are negative labels, so all the labels y, yi = - 1 here and yi = +1 for these points, these are two dimensional

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: = +1 for these points, these are two dimensional data.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: Now, if you solve the regression problem let us say you get some w which looks like this, now what does that mean, that means that well this is the hw that you are going to get and anybody on the right hand side is going to be positive, anybody on the left hand side is going to be negative, it looks good. So, this is an in fact a good way to separate the positives from the

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: a good way to separate the positives from the negatives and your regression might give you this.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: Now, let us say I add a bunch of extra points. So, I add maybe a bunch of points here. So, now the moment I add these points in terms of classification these points are not, should not really bother our line here that we have found out, because these points are still on the correct side of the line so if you had run an algorithm, a classification algorithm it might still get you

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: a classification algorithm it might still get you the same line, so which separates the positives from the negatives.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: But now regression is going to not just minimize the 0-1 loss, but then it is trying to minimize the average deviation between w<sup>T</sup> x and the corresponding y, the y for all of these are going to be +1 but then if I use this w, the w<sup>T</sup> x is going to be large. So, it is going to be positive but then regression does not just look at the sign, it is going to look at

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: not just look at the sign, it is going to look at the exact value and these guys will have a large w<sup>T</sup> x and so w<sup>T</sup> x - the label 1 square will be large for these.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: And so to balance this, to minimize the loss function what it is going to do it is kind of going to tilt my w, so maybe the w that I will get will get slightly tilted and it might become something like this. So, my w might tilt in this direction or maybe I will use a different color or I will use a dotted version of the w, maybe my w will tilt in this direction and then I might

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: my w will tilt in this direction and then I might get some line like this. So, now let us say if you had some points here as well.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, now what is happening is that your w gets tilted back and forth just because there are these points which are far apart from the line, from the line, from the separator. So, now for a classification point of view it does not really matter if my point is far apart from the line as long as it is on the correct side of the line.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, but from a regression point of view you are trying to minimize the average prediction minus the label square. So, the label you are going to think of it as some real number it so happens in this data regression will think that it so happens in this data the labels the real numbers are happened to be +1 or -1 and so it will try to match +1 or -1 as much as possible and so it

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: to match +1 or -1 as much as possible and so it will try to shift the predictor itself.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: And that is a bad thing, that might lead us to some line which might actually make errors on the data set while you can actually find a line which makes no errors in this case in terms of the 0-1 loss. But whereas the line that minimizes the squared loss may end up making some errors.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: In other words, the main point is that your least squares or the regression problem. So, conclusion that we draw from this is that regression is sensitive to the location of the data points and not just, I am loosely writing, not just the “side” of on which the data points lie with respect to the separator.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, by side I mean that the w<sup>T</sup> x, the sign (w<sup>T</sup> x) is what should matter whereas it is actually looking at the value of w<sup>T</sup> x, so that is the problem with regression. So, it is not a great idea to directly take regression and apply to classification and hope that it will give you good results in the test set.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, now how do we solve this? So, then it so we are stuck, seems like we are stuck because we put down loss function which is the most natural loss function for classification, we said that the original problem is NP-hard to solve, we cannot use regression also, so we need to come up with alternate algorithms.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: And one of the main reasons why we have so many algorithms for binary classification that we will see throughout this course is simply because of the fact that this problem, original problem that we want to solve is actually hard. So, you somehow have to get around this hardness in different ways by making different assumptions and that will lead us to different different

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: and that will lead us to different different algorithms, so that is the main idea why you have so many classification algorithms,

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: whereas you have like a very standard regression algorithm which is the least squares regression.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, what we are going to do in the next couple of classes, the next couple of lectures is going to look at a lot of classification algorithms. But just to warm up we will start with the simplest possible classification algorithms and then we will make it slightly more complicated not just for the sake of making it complicated but because it will become more and more principled as

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: it will become more and more principled as we go along in this course. But for now what we want to do is start with the simplest possible classification algorithm.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: So, here is a question for you, so pause and think, forgetting all that we have discussed so far if you had to do a classification problem, solve a classification problem, you have a data set, you are given a new data point. What is the simplest way that you will use to make a prediction of +1 or -1 or 0 or 1 for this new data point that I give you. Think about this and then we

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: that I give you. Think about this and then we will come and I mean we will start discussing this problem now.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Binary Classification**

text: Hello. So, far in supervised learning, we have looked at binary classification. And we have looked at the problem of K nearest neighbors, and the algorithm called K nearest neighbors to solve the problem of binary classification, we identified an important problem with K nearest neighbors, which is the fact that K nearest neighbors does not learn any model from data.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: So, we asked the question, can we come up with algorithms, which given a dataset learn some interesting model from this data, and once the model is learned, we can throw away the dataset and only use the model to make predictions on test data points.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Today, now, what we are going to see is such an algorithm, which is a simple algorithm, very popular algorithm, and it is it kind of is a base algorithm for several complicated algorithms that we will see later on as well.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: And this algorithm is what is called as the decision tree algorithm. What is the input to this algorithm? As usual, the input is a data set, which is a training data set, which looks like as usual, (x1, y1), dot, dot, dot, (xn, yn) where xi is in d dimension for all data points. And yi is in +1 or -1, this is a binary classification problem.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Now, what is going to be the output of this algorithm is? You take the dataset and then output a decision tree. And this decision tree is going to be used to make future computation future predictions. So, what is a decision tree?

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: A decision tree is going to look as follows. How is the decision tree going to look like? It is a tree, a tree in the sense of computer science, tree is going to look like following. So, it is a binary tree. And it is going to have some nodes, it is a root tree, it is going to have a root node. And then you are going to have some internal nodes, and then you are going to have some

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: nodes, and then you are going to have some leaf nodes, that is our tree typically is.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: So, that in every node, you are going to ask a question. Let us say this is question 1. So, this is a yes or no question, which means it has an answer to this question can be either yes or no? And now, if the answer is yes, then you ask a different question, question 2, if the answer is no, then perhaps you ask a different question, question 3, and so on. Now, what might happen

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: question 3, and so on. Now, what might happen is, if the question is, question 2 can have again 2 answers, yes or no. And now if it can still go on, so maybe you increase the number of questions, maybe question 4, I am just giving you an example, question 5.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Whereas question 3 has two answers, yes or no. But then you do not further ask any questions. You make a decision here. The green boxes are decision boxes. And what might be the decision? The decision in this case is a 1 and in this case, let us say some -1, or for this algorithm, let us say it is 0. So, we can simply does not really matter, we can, but I am just going to use 0 to

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: matter, we can, but I am just going to use 0 to indicate negative samples.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Whereas in the other side, you ask extra 2 questions. And then may, based on that, you might make a decision, this might be yes or no, this is a yes no. And now you make the decisions maybe here it is 1, here it is 0, here it is 0 here it is 1. So, these nodes, these guys are called as prediction or leaf nodes. So, this is the tree.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: So, this is we will talk more about what exactly are these questions and so on. But this is the structure of the tree. It is a binary tree. It is gone every root, every node internal node, all the red boxes are internal nodes, every internal node is has a question associated with it. And then there are 2 answers to it. And the green nodes are the leaf nodes of the prediction

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: green nodes are the leaf nodes of the prediction nodes. So, now how would we predict? The prediction is easy. The prediction is going to be as follows.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: So, somehow, let us say somebody gives you this tree that they have constructed from the data set. Now you are given a new test data point, let us say x test. Now, all you do is traverse through the tree to reach a leaf node. Now, y test is the value in the leaf node. What do I mean by that? I mean that, you pass the test data point x test to this tree and ask the series of

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: point x test to this tree and ask the series of questions and then if the questions answer for the x test data points that you pass as no, then you go to the right side if it is yes, you go to the left side, which means that you are tracing out a path in this tree. And every path ends on a leaf node. And the leaf node has a corresponding 1 or 0 associated with it. And then that

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: 1 or 0 associated with it. And then that would be the label that we predict.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: So, basically, what we have done is, we are creating a series of questions from our data set. And these questions are enough, we say to make predictions of test data points. So, the model here, which was lacking in a K nearest neighbor problem, the model is the tree. Once I have this tree, I do not need the data set anymore. This tree encodes all the information that I need for

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: tree encodes all the information that I need for prediction that was available in my data set.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Once I have this, I can throw away the data set and work just with the tree, which is enough to make future predictions for any given data point. So, that is why this is a powerful thing. Because the prediction is fast. So, you just have to traverse through the trees, answer a bunch of questions, and then outcomes the answer.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: So, now the questions that we have to ask is, what are these questions? So, how do I mean how do I build this tree is one thing, but even before we build the tree, we need to ask ourselves, what are we? What type of questions are we asking? So, that we can think about how to build the tree in an efficient way? So, what is the question?

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Question? In a binary tree, in a decision tree, a question you can think of a question as a feature value pair. What does that mean? An example would be feature is height, let us say height and the value is 180 centimeters. Now, the question that we will create out of this is as follows; is the height ≤ 180 centimeters? Maybe this is feature 3, maybe this is some value θ. So, in

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: is feature 3, maybe this is some value θ. So, in general, it is some feature and then we are asking is some feature less than or equal to some value? This is the type of questions that we are going to restrict our tree to have.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: So, once we know what kind of questions we are going to ask, then we need to build this tree somehow from the dataset. Now, which means we have to, what we are going to do is? We are going to greedily ask questions. So, we have to first somehow find what is the best question to ask? And then we will ask, we will later on try to expand this tree. So, which means we need to somehow

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: this tree. So, which means we need to somehow have a measure of how good a question is? So, what we need immediately then is? We have to ask ourselves how to measure goodness of question?

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: So, what might be a good way? So, this might be a good time to pause and think, how do you, how can you measure goodness of a question? So, just to just to, point you in the, perhaps a useful direction to think about, instead ask the question, what would be the property of the best possible question? So, I have a data set. And my goal is to do prediction well.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Now, what would be really good question? So, what property should that question have? let us ask this ourselves.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Now, what is a question do to our data set? So, remember, we have this data set, let us call this D. So, data set, which is just a bunch of data points of this form (x1, y1), dot dot dot,(xn, yn). And now we ask a question, let us take one particular question, some feature, let us say height is less than or equal to some value, let us say 100, 120 centimeters, 130 centimeters,

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: let us say 100, 120 centimeters, 130 centimeters, whatever. So, we ask this question, of course, this is a yes or no question.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Now, there are, I can ask this question to every data point in my data set. So, and there are two answers yes and no. Now, some data points will have features where the height <180 centimeters, and some data points will have the feature height > 180 centimeters. So, which means that this question is dividing my dataset into two parts.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: So, there is one part which is D yes, I am going to call this the D yes part, which is just a dataset with maybe the first point answered yes, maybe the 10th point answered yes, (x10,y10) dot dot dot some bunch of points. So, it is a yes or no question. So, every point either answers yes or no. So, which means there is a D no also, which has the remaining set of points (x2, y2),

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: which has the remaining set of points (x2, y2), (x3,y3), dot, dot, dot, and so on. So, it is, you are separating, you are partitioning your data set into two parts, where one part answered yes to this question the other part answered no to this question.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Now, what would be an ideal question? A really good question should have what property? The best possible question. It should have the property that I should be able to make prediction by just asking this question. Which means when will I be able to make a solid prediction by asking just this question? In my training data, if I asked this question, if height < 180 centimeters, and

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: this question, if height < 180 centimeters, and I observed that every data point which answers yes, has the corresponding label as +1 and every data point that answers no has the corresponding the label -1 or the other way around.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: Every data point that answers yes has the corresponding label -1 and every data point that answers no has the corresponding label +1, then I know that this is a super good question, because by asking the single question, I am able to distinguish the +1 from the -1. Everybody answered, yes, have the same label and everybody answers no have the same label, then this is a very good

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: no have the same label, then this is a very good question. Because this one question is enough to, classify whether the data point is +1 or -1 in my training set, which means that I will hope to do the same thing for my test data point also.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: If the test data point comes, I will just look at the height. If it is < 180 centimeters, then I will say maybe +1, and otherwise, I will say -1, depending on what the dataset says. Now, that is an ideal world. So, where you have a single super good question, which is enough to say, divide my data set into two parts, where the labels of one side is the same, and the labels of the

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: of one side is the same, and the labels of the other side is also the same.

metadata.h1: **Machine Learning Techniques Professor Arun RajKumar Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Madras Introduction to Decision Trees**

text: But in reality, such a question may not exist, there may not be a single question that you can ask such that you can divide your data sets into pure sets. So, when I say pure, it means that the labels on each of the sides are pure, where exactly the same. Such pure questions may not exist.
